exemplar.title: Should Coca-Cola reconsider its approach to sustainability?

detailedExemplarData.title: Should Coca-Cola reconsider its approach to sustainability?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Key concept of sustainability clearly identified and foregrounded, providing a strong thematic anchor (Page 1)
Succinct definition of sustainability underpins subsequent analysis (Page 3)
Introduction of greenwashing effectively links consumer perception to sustainability (Page 4)
Application of the circular business model demonstrates strong integration into strategic recommendations (Page 8)
weaknesses: Historical background lacks a clear link to sustainability focus (Page 3)
Some financial passages drift away from the sustainability concept
Sustainability themes could be woven more systematically across all SWOT quadrants for balance (Page 7)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits strong understanding and application of the key concept throughout most sections, with clear analytical links to Coca-Colaâs sustainability issues. Occasional drift in financial discussion and uneven integration in some SWOT quadrants prevent a topâband score, so 4/5 reflects consistent yet imperfect integration.

strengths: Five supporting documents are cited, meeting the 3â5 requirement
Documents (annual report, NGO petition, competitor data) are relevant and add numerical depth
Custom circular business model diagram is well-designed and informative (Page 10)
weaknesses: Lacks critical evaluation of source reliability or potential bias (Page 3)
References are often superficial with limited interrogation of data tables
Depth of analysis is moderate rather than demonstrating a broad range of ideas
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Quantity and relevance of documents satisfy the criteria for 3 marks, and while they add useful evidence, the superficial treatment and absence of source critique prevent reaching the highest band.

strengths: Adequate selection of SWOT, STEEPLE and circular model tools applied to Coca-Cola (Page 5)
Tools are linked to the research question and organizational context
Strategic recommendations (e.g., capital reallocation to waste reduction) demonstrate application of theory to practice
weaknesses: Analysis tends to be descriptive rather than deeply analytical
Omission of more sophisticated financial tools (e.g., ratios, forecasts) limits depth
Rationale for tool selection could be further elaborated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The choice and use of tools are adequate and clearly connected to the research question, but the level of analytical depth and justification for tool selection remain in the mid-band, warranting 3/4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Use of quantitative data (water usage, net cash from operations) effectively grounds analysis
Discussion of automated manufacturing efficiency shows strong understanding of technological factors (Page 8)
Step-by-step outline of circular system collection enhances feasibility discussion (Page 11)
Reflection on potential source bias demonstrates critical evaluation (Page 12)
weaknesses: Analytical depth is uneven and lacks triangulation of data
Speculative claims (e.g., bankruptcy from plastic bans) are unsupported by cost scenarios
Many STEEPLE observations are general and not explicitly tied to the research question
Missing cost estimates or modelling to substantiate feasibility of circular model
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis and evaluation are present and link evidence to strategic options, but uneven depth, unsupported speculation, and lack of detailed modelling limit sustained, thorough analysis, placing it at 3/5.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question and highlights greenwashing concerns (Page 12)
Justification is drawn from the main analysis, maintaining overall coherence
weaknesses: Some assertions (e.g., bankruptcy or prohibitive costs) are not fully supported by evidence
Would benefit from a clearer set of prioritized recommendations or next steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion addresses the research question and is generally consistent with the evidence, but unsupported assertions and lack of clear prioritization prevent full consistency with the highest band, so 2/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Logical and coherent structure with title page, contents, and clearly labelled sections
Comprehensive table of contents enhances navigation and clarity
weaknesses: Contents could use further sub-headings or indentation to reflect subsections
Narrative could more explicitly reference visual models for easier mapping
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The document demonstrates an appropriate organizational structure throughout, fulfilling the criteria for 2 marks, with only minor improvements suggested.

strengths: All required presentation elements are included (research question, key concept, word count, candidate code, citations)
Adheres to IB submission guidelines
weaknesses: Inconsistent footnote formatting between STEEPLE and SWOT sections
Minor formatting inconsistencies detract slightly from overall polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully meets the requirements for inclusion of elements, meriting the top band of 2 marks, with only minor formatting refinements needed.

strandSelections.reasoning: A logical structure with title page, contents, labelled sections and sub-headings is evident throughout, meeting expectations for appropriate structure (2 marks).

The documents (annual report, NGO petition, competitor data, etc.) are relevant and add some numerical depth, but most are referenced superficially; the student rarely interrogates reliability or provides detailed data tables.  This corresponds to ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than the breadth and depth demanded for the top band.
The submission includes the research question, key concept, word count, candidate code and citations, satisfying the requirement that all elements be present.
In-text citations indicate five numbered supporting documents, meeting the 3â5 requirement.  This fits the 3-mark band for quantity.

Each tool is applied to Coca-Cola, and links are drawn to the research question, but comments are often descriptive (âcould damage brand imageâ) instead of deeply analytical, so relevance is clear but effectiveness is mixedâconsistent with the 3-mark band.

The student selects some financial and environmental statistics and integrates them into discussion (e.g., water-usage megalitres, advertising spend).  Data are adequate in scope but not fully exploited or triangulated, placing the work in the middle band.

Analysis goes beyond narrative by linking findings to stakeholder reactions and strategic options, but depth is uneven and often lacks evaluation of feasibility or long-term impact.  There is some integration of evidence, yet not the sustained, thorough analysis of the top levels.

The research question is directly answeredââCoca-Cola may want to reconsider its approach to sustainabilityââwith justification drawn from the analysis, fulfilling the descriptor for the top band in this strand.

SWOT, STEEPLE, simple financial figures and a circular model are includedâan adequate range for an HL IA, though not especially sophisticated (no ratios, BCG, or quantitative forecasts).  This matches the âadequate selectionâ description.

The conclusion generally aligns with the evidence presented, but some assertions about possible bankruptcy or cost impossibility are not well supported by data, creating partial rather than full consistency.  Hence the mid-band is appropriate.

Sustainability issues are constantly tied back to Coca-Cola through figures on water use, advertising spend, stakeholder pressure and comparisons with PepsiCo.  The SWOT/-STEEPLE tables and the circular model are explicitly applied to the organisation, demonstrating strong but not flawless integration; some generic statements (âcustomers may leave the companyâ) lack firm organisational evidence.  This aligns with the âstrong analysis with partial integrationâ descriptor.

The student defines sustainability, repeatedly links it to water usage, plastic waste and green-washing, and uses the concept to frame SWOT, STEEPLE and the circular model.  The discussion is more than a simple mention and shows analytical consideration of HOW sustainability shapes Coca-Colaâs risks and options.  However, integration is not seamless across every sectionâsome financial passages drift away from the conceptâso it falls short of the fully-integrated standard required for the top band.

annotations.comment: The threats quadrant repeats speculative bankruptcy language; refine with clearer linkage to specific policy changes or regulatory scenarios.
The discussion of automated manufacturing efficiency is well-developed, showing understanding of technological factors in sustainability.
The research question is clearly stated but could be refined for specificity, for example by defining a timeframe or geographic focus to strengthen its scope.
The key concept of sustainability is clearly identified and foregrounded, providing a strong thematic anchor for the entire assessment.
All required presentation elements (research question, key concept, word count, candidate code) are included, demonstrating adherence to IB submission guidelines.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well structured, enhancing navigation and clarity throughout the document.
Consider adding sub-headings or indentation in the contents to reflect major subsections (e.g. financial versus tools analysis) for even clearer structure.
The historical background is descriptive but lacks a clear link to the sustainability focus; consider framing early context in terms of environmental or social implications.
The definition of sustainability is succinct and provides a clear foundation for linking subsequent analysis to the key concept.
The introduction cites supporting documents but does not comment on their reliability or potential bias; critically evaluating sources will deepen the analysis.
Use of quantitative data (net cash from operations) effectively grounds the findings in evidence, supporting subsequent strategic discussion.
The claim that a ban on single-use plastic could bankrupt Coca-Cola is speculative; include cost scenarios or modelling to substantiate this threat assessment.
The section clearly introduces greenwashing to contextualise stakeholder concerns, demonstrating integration of sustainability with consumer perception analysis.
The discussion of brand image and World Without Waste is descriptive; consider evaluating impacts using consumer sentiment metrics or cost-benefit ratios.
Incorporating a ratio such as sustainability investment as a percentage of operating cash would deepen the financial analysis and tool application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The recommendation to reallocate capital towards waste reduction in production demonstrates strategic application of theory to practice.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The rationale for selecting STEEPLE and SWOT is concise but could be extended by explaining why other models (e.g. BCG, Porterâs Five Forces) were excluded.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Many STEEPLE entries are general observations; each should be explicitly linked back to Coca-Colaâs sustainability research question to increase relevance.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent between STEEPLE and SWOT sections; standardising footnote style would improve presentation coherence.
SWOT section is clearly labelled and improves the documentâs logical flow by marking a new analytical phase.
Sustainability themes appear in strengths and weaknesses but could be woven more systematically across all four SWOT quadrants for balanced analysis.
The link to the circular business model here demonstrates strong application of the key concept within strategic recommendations.
The custom circular business model diagram is well-designed and effectively communicates process improvements.
The step-by-step collection process is clearly outlined, enhancing the feasibility discussion of implementing a circular system.
Comparing manual versus automated sorting with cost estimates would provide a more rigorous evaluation of the proposed process.
Estimating potential waste reduction volume or percentage would strengthen the argument for the circular modelâs impact.
The conclusion succinctly highlights greenwashing concerns and aligns closely with the research questionâs focus.
Acknowledgement of cost limitations is appropriate but would benefit from rough cost projections to gauge feasibility.
Reflection on potential source bias is commendable and demonstrates critical evaluation of supporting documents.
Consider concluding with a concise set of prioritized recommendations or next steps to provide clearer guidance.
The claim of being the worst plastic polluter requires comparative data or a citation of the ranking methodology to validate its inclusion.
The recommendation to adopt a circular business model directly addresses sustainability issues and advances the argument for operational change.
Competitor analysis is insightful but would benefit from quantitative comparison (e.g. PepsiCoâs sustainability spend or emission reductions).
To improve clarity, explicitly reference and annotate each model stage in the text so readers can easily map narrative to visual.
